screens.prompt: Every recording is finite — you can only ever feed the periodogram a chopped-off piece of the burst, as if you'd multiplied the true (infinite) signal by a rectangular "on/off" window. Does that truncation itself distort the spectrum, even before any noise is involved?
choices: No — cutting a clean sinusoid off after a few cycles still gives a clean spectral line
Yes — energy that should sit at one frequency spreads into nearby frequencies
screens.reveal: Truncating a signal in time is multiplication by a rectangular window, and multiplication in time is *convolution* in frequency. Convolving a single spectral line with the rectangular window's own spectrum (a wide, ringing sinc) smears that line's energy across neighboring frequencies — **spectral leakage**. It happens to a perfectly clean tone with zero noise, purely because the recording has a start and an end.

screens.prompt: A smoother taper (e.g. a Hann window) instead of a hard rectangular cutoff reduces leakage into distant frequencies. What's the cost?

choices.text: The main spectral peak gets wider — you trade frequency resolution for lower leakage
Nothing — smoother windows are strictly better with no downside
choices.feedback: Every window is a tradeoff. A Hann window's main lobe is roughly twice as wide as the rectangular window's, which is exactly the resolution you're giving up.

choices.text: It removes the need for averaging multiple segments later
choices.feedback: Windowing addresses leakage, not the periodogram's fundamental variance problem from the previous lesson — those are two separate issues, and Bartlett/Welch averaging (next lesson) is still needed for the variance.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — every window shape trades main-lobe width (resolution) against side-lobe height (leakage suppression). There's no free lunch, only a choice of where on that tradeoff you sit.
